healthy.label: Healthy
healthy.message: No Disease Detected. Crop is Healthy.

cercospora_leaf_spot.cause_of_disease: Caused by the fungus Cercospora zeae-maydis, thriving in warm, humid conditions with heavy dew and temperatures between 22–30°C. Spreads through infected crop debris left in the field. Rectangular, grayish-tan lesions run parallel to leaf veins, reducing photosynthesis and yield.
deficiencies: Nitrogen Deficiency — weakens overall plant immunity making it highly susceptible to fungal attack; shows as yellowing of lower leaves
Potassium Deficiency — reduces cell wall strength and plant's natural disease resistance; increases lesion spread
Silicon Deficiency — low silicon levels weaken leaf surface structure making fungal penetration easier
disorders: Premature Leaf Senescence — infected leaves die early reducing the plant's ability to photosynthesize
Reduced Grain Fill — early leaf loss during grain-filling stage directly reduces kernel weight and yield
Stalk Weakening — severe infections weaken the stalk making plants prone to lodging and breakage
natural_organic_treatment: Spray Neem Oil 3000 PPM @ 3 ml per litre of water at first symptom — repeat every 10 days
Apply Trichoderma viride @ 4 g per litre of water as a foliar spray for biological fungal control
Use Bacillus subtilis bio-fungicide @ 2 g per litre — effective as both preventive and curative spray
Remove and destroy all infected crop debris after harvest to eliminate fungal overwintering source
Maintain proper crop rotation — avoid planting maize in the same field consecutively for 2 seasons
treatment: Apply Propiconazole 25% EC or Azoxystrobin 23% SC fungicide immediately
Remove and destroy infected lower leaves
Improve air circulation by maintaining proper plant spacing
Avoid overhead irrigation to reduce leaf wetness
Rotate crops to reduce fungal buildup in soil

common_rust.cause_of_disease: Caused by Puccinia sorghi, spreading rapidly through airborne spores in cool, moist weather between 16–23°C. Small, circular to elongated powdery brick-red pustules appear on both leaf surfaces. High humidity and dew on leaves accelerate infection.
deficiencies: Potassium Deficiency — weakens cell walls and reduces the plant's natural resistance to rust pustule formation
Silicon Deficiency — low silicon content in leaves makes them structurally weak and easier for rust spores to penetrate
Zinc Deficiency — impairs plant immune signalling and makes recovery from rust infection slower
disorders: Reduced Grain Fill — heavy rust infection during grain-fill stage leads to poor kernel development and lightweight grains
Premature Leaf Death — severe rust causes early leaf chlorosis and death reducing overall photosynthetic capacity
Increased Susceptibility to Secondary Infections — rust-weakened tissue invites secondary fungal and bacterial pathogens
natural_organic_treatment: Spray Neem Oil 3000 PPM @ 3 ml per litre every 10–14 days from first pustule appearance
Apply Baking Soda (Sodium Bicarbonate) solution @ 5 g per litre of water with a few drops of liquid soap — disrupts spore germination
Use Bacillus subtilis bio-fungicide spray @ 2 g per litre as a preventive and early curative spray
Apply wood ash around the base of plants to improve potassium levels and strengthen plant resistance
Use resistant hybrid varieties in next season to naturally prevent rust recurrence
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP or Propiconazole 25% EC immediately at first pustule sighting
Avoid overhead irrigation as it spreads spores
Remove heavily infected leaves to reduce spore load
Use rust-resistant hybrid varieties in next season
Ensure proper field sanitation after harvest

northern_leaf_blight.cause_of_disease: Caused by Exserohilum turcicum, producing large cigar-shaped lesions (2.5–15 cm long) in warm, humid conditions between 18–27°C. Fungal spores overwinter in infected crop debris and spread via wind and rain splashes. Disease starts from lower leaves and moves upward.
deficiencies: Nitrogen Deficiency — excess or deficient nitrogen both promote disease; deficient nitrogen weakens the plant's recovery ability
Potassium Deficiency — reduces the plant's ability to resist fungal penetration and slows wound healing in infected tissue
Calcium Deficiency — weakens cell wall integrity allowing faster lesion expansion across leaf tissue
disorders: Premature Canopy Collapse — large cigar-shaped lesions rapidly destroy the leaf canopy reducing photosynthesis by up to 50%
Poor Kernel Development — early canopy loss during silking and grain-fill stage directly causes shrivelled, lightweight kernels
Increased Stalk Rot Risk — plants weakened by leaf blight become highly prone to secondary stalk rot fungi like Fusarium
natural_organic_treatment: Spray Trichoderma harzianum @ 4 g per litre of water as a foliar biological fungicide — apply at tasseling stage
Apply Neem Oil 3000 PPM @ 3 ml per litre every 10–14 days from disease onset
Use Copper-based organic spray (Bordeaux mixture 1%) @ 500 ml per litre diluted in 200 litres — natural copper fungicide
Apply Pseudomonas fluorescens bio-agent @ 5 g per litre as a foliar spray for biological disease suppression
Burn and destroy all infected crop debris immediately after harvest to break the disease cycle
treatment: Apply Propiconazole 25% EC or Azoxystrobin + Propiconazole combo immediately
Apply at tasseling to silking stage — this is the most critical treatment window
Remove and burn infected plant debris after harvest
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which promotes disease spread
Use resistant varieties for future planting

healthy.label: Healthy
healthy.message: No Disease Detected. Crop is Healthy.

aphids.cause_of_disease: Caused by Aphis gossypii (cotton aphid), a small soft-bodied insect clustering on leaf undersides and growing tips. Thrives in warm, dry conditions between 20–30°C. Spreads rapidly through winged adults and transmits viral diseases. Secretes honeydew leading to sooty mold.
deficiencies: Nitrogen Deficiency Symptoms — aphid sap-sucking causes yellowing and stunting that mimics nitrogen deficiency in affected leaves
Magnesium Deficiency — prolonged aphid feeding reduces plant's ability to absorb and utilize magnesium leading to interveinal chlorosis
Zinc Deficiency — heavy infestation weakens root function impairing zinc uptake causing small distorted new leaves
disorders: Sooty Mold Disorder — honeydew secreted by aphids encourages black sooty mold growth blocking sunlight and reducing photosynthesis
Leaf Curl Disorder — toxins injected during aphid feeding cause leaf curling, cupping, and distortion of young shoots
Viral Transmission Disorder — aphids act as vectors transmitting Cotton Leaf Curl Virus (CLCuV) causing irreversible stunting
natural_organic_treatment: Spray Neem Oil 3000 PPM @ 5 ml per litre of water — suffocates aphids and disrupts their life cycle; repeat every 7 days
Apply Insecticidal Soap (1% solution) — spray directly on aphid colonies on leaf undersides to kill on contact
Release Ladybugs (Coccinella septempunctata) or Green Lacewings in the field — natural biological predators of aphids
Spray Garlic Extract solution @ 50 g crushed garlic boiled in 1 litre water, diluted 1:10 — natural aphid repellent
Place Yellow Sticky Traps @ 10–12 per acre to monitor and reduce winged aphid population naturally
treatment: Apply Imidacloprid 17.8% SL or Thiamethoxam 25% WG immediately
Spray during early morning or evening for best absorption
Place yellow sticky traps for monitoring and mass trapping
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which attracts aphids
Rotate insecticide groups to prevent resistance buildup

army_worm.cause_of_disease: Caused by larvae of Spodoptera species. Active at night, hiding in soil during daytime. Thrives in warm, humid conditions. Spreads through adult moth flight and egg-laying. Can devastate entire fields within days if undetected causing complete defoliation.
deficiencies: Nitrogen Deficiency — severe defoliation by army worms deprives plants of leaf nitrogen causing systemic starvation and poor recovery
Phosphorus Deficiency — root damage from soil-dwelling larvae reduces phosphorus absorption leading to dark purplish discoloration of leaves
Iron Deficiency — larval feeding damage disrupts the plant's vascular system reducing iron transport to new growth
disorders: Complete Defoliation Disorder — mass larval feeding strips the plant of all leaves leaving bare stalks unable to photosynthesize
Boll Shedding Disorder — feeding damage at boll attachment points causes premature boll drop reducing lint yield
Root Zone Damage — soil-dwelling early instar larvae damage feeder roots causing wilting and nutrient uptake failure
natural_organic_treatment: Spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) var. kurstaki @ 1 kg per acre — highly effective biological insecticide that kills larvae naturally; apply in evening
Apply Neem Oil 3000 PPM @ 5 ml per litre — acts as antifeedant preventing larvae from feeding; spray in the evening
Use Nuclear Polyhedrosis Virus (NPV) of Spodoptera @ 250 LE per acre — species-specific biological control agent
Set up Pheromone Traps @ 5–6 per acre to capture adult moths and reduce egg-laying significantly
Deep plow the field after harvest to expose and kill pupae and eggs in the soil naturally
treatment: Apply Chlorpyrifos 20% EC or Emamectin Benzoate 5% SG immediately
Spray in the evening or early morning when larvae are most active
Remove and destroy egg masses manually during early infestation
Use pheromone traps for monitoring adult moth population
Deep plowing after harvest to expose and kill pupae in soil

medication_timeline.when_to_apply: When larvae are in early instar stage
When larvae are in early instar stage
When larvae are in early instar stage

bacterial_blight.cause_of_disease: Caused by Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. malvacearum, thriving in warm, wet conditions between 25–35°C. Spreads through rain splash, contaminated farm tools, infected seeds, and working in wet fields. Water-soaked angular spots on leaves turning brown/black are the first visible symptoms.
deficiencies: Copper Deficiency — low copper levels reduce plant's natural antibacterial defense mechanism making it prone to bacterial infection
Calcium Deficiency — weakens cell walls allowing bacterial penetration and rapid spread through leaf tissue
Boron Deficiency — impairs cell wall integrity and reduces the plant's ability to seal off infected tissue and prevent spread
disorders: Boll Rot Disorder — bacteria invade developing bolls causing internal rotting and complete loss of lint and seed
Vascular Wilting Disorder — bacterial invasion of stem vascular tissue causes water and nutrient transport blockage leading to wilting
Fiber Quality Disorder — infected bolls produce discolored, weak fiber with significantly reduced commercial value
natural_organic_treatment: Apply Bordeaux Mixture (1%) @ 500 ml diluted in 200 litres — copper-based organic bactericide; spray at first symptom
Treat seeds in Hot Water @ 57°C for 30 minutes before sowing to eliminate seed-borne bacterial infection
Spray Garlic Extract @ 100 g crushed garlic in 1 litre water diluted 1:5 — natural broad-spectrum antibacterial spray
Apply Trichoderma viride @ 4 g per litre as soil drenching to suppress bacterial activity in the root zone
Use compost tea spray (well-decomposed compost steeped in water for 24 hours) to promote beneficial microbes that suppress pathogens
treatment: Apply Copper Oxychloride 50% WP or Streptomycin + Tetracycline combo immediately
Avoid working in the field when it is wet to prevent bacterial spread
Remove and destroy infected leaves and bolls
Use disease-free certified seeds for future planting
Disinfect farm tools regularly with bleach solution

powdery_mildew.cause_of_disease: Caused by Leveillula taurica, favored by warm days, cool nights, and dry conditions between 20–28°C. Spreads through airborne spores. White to grayish powdery growth develops on upper and lower leaf surfaces. Excessive nitrogen fertilization and dense planting promote rapid spread.
deficiencies: Silicon Deficiency — low silicon content weakens epidermal cell walls making leaves highly susceptible to powdery mildew penetration
Calcium Deficiency — reduces cell wall strength allowing fungal haustoria to penetrate leaf cells more easily
Potassium Deficiency — imbalanced potassium levels reduce osmotic regulation in leaf cells making them vulnerable to fungal colonization
disorders: Premature Leaf Drop Disorder — infected leaves yellow rapidly and drop early reducing photosynthetic area and boll filling capacity
Reduced Boll Set Disorder — early defoliation from mildew weakens the plant causing excessive shedding of immature bolls
Physiological Drought Stress — powdery mildew blocks stomata disrupting transpiration and increasing internal heat stress in the plant
natural_organic_treatment: Spray Neem Oil 3000 PPM @ 5 ml per litre every 7–10 days — disrupts fungal spore germination on leaf surfaces
Apply Baking Soda (Sodium Bicarbonate) @ 5–10 g per litre with 2 ml liquid soap — changes leaf surface pH inhibiting fungal growth
Spray Milk solution (1 part fresh milk : 9 parts water) — milk proteins act as natural fungicide effective against powdery mildew
Use Wettable Sulphur (organic grade) @ 3 g per litre — natural contact fungicide effective against powdery mildew
Apply Trichoderma viride @ 4 g per litre as a foliar spray for biological suppression of the fungal pathogen
treatment: Apply Sulphur 80% WP or Hexaconazole 5% EC immediately
Ensure good air circulation through proper plant spacing
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves
Do not spray Sulphur when temperatures exceed 35°C as it may cause phytotoxicity

medication_timeline.when_to_apply: At first sign of white powdery growth
At first sign of white powdery growth
At first sign of white powdery growth

target_spot.cause_of_disease: Caused by Corynespora cassiicola, thriving in high humidity above 80% and temperatures between 25–32°C. Spreads through airborne spores and rain splash. Circular brown lesions with concentric rings give a target-like appearance. Dense canopy conditions that trap moisture significantly accelerate spread.
deficiencies: Potassium Deficiency — reduces plant resistance to fungal diseases; potassium-deficient plants show faster lesion expansion and spread
Magnesium Deficiency — weakens chloroplast function reducing the plant's photosynthetic recovery capacity after infection
Zinc Deficiency — slows tissue repair mechanisms and reduces the plant's ability to form defensive compounds against the fungus
disorders: Premature Defoliation Disorder — target spot lesions cause rapid leaf yellowing and drop dramatically reducing photosynthesis
Boll Retention Failure — severe defoliation triggers a stress response in the plant causing immature boll shedding
Secondary Infection Disorder — open lesions created by target spot create entry points for secondary bacterial and fungal pathogens
natural_organic_treatment: Spray Neem Oil 3000 PPM @ 5 ml per litre every 10–14 days from disease onset — reduces fungal spore germination
Apply Trichoderma viride bio-fungicide @ 4 g per litre as foliar spray — effective biological control of Corynespora
Use Copper-based Bordeaux Mixture (0.5–1%) spray — organic copper fungicide with broad-spectrum activity
Apply compost mulch around plant base to reduce rain splash which spreads spores from soil to leaves
Maintain proper plant spacing and remove lower leaves to improve air circulation and reduce humidity in the canopy
treatment: Apply Azoxystrobin 23% SC or Thiophanate Methyl 70% WP immediately
Improve canopy airflow through proper plant spacing
Avoid excessive irrigation especially on foliage
Remove and destroy infected leaves promptly
Apply preventive fungicide sprays during high humidity periods

healthy.label: Healthy
healthy.message: No Disease Detected. Crop is Healthy.

blast.cause_of_disease: Caused by Magnaporthe oryzae, the most destructive rice disease worldwide. Thrives in cool, humid conditions between 20–28°C with heavy dew or rain. Diamond/eye-shaped lesions on leaves (leaf blast) and blackening of neck (neck blast). Excess nitrogen and dense planting worsen the disease.
deficiencies: Silicon Deficiency — most critical deficiency; low silicon dramatically increases blast susceptibility as silicon strengthens leaf cell walls against fungal penetration
Potassium Deficiency — weakens plant immunity and reduces the ability to compartmentalize blast lesions preventing their spread
Phosphorus Deficiency — slows root development reducing the plant's overall vigor and disease tolerance capacity
disorders: Neck Rot Disorder — blast infection at the panicle neck completely cuts off nutrient flow causing the entire panicle to turn white (called white ear)
Panicle Sterility Disorder — neck blast at early grain-fill stage causes complete spikelet sterility resulting in empty grains and near-total yield loss
Node Blast Disorder — infection at stem nodes causes dark brown to black node discoloration and structural weakening leading to plant collapse
natural_organic_treatment: Apply Silicon-rich organic amendment (rice husk ash) @ 200 kg per acre as soil application — significantly boosts natural blast resistance
Spray Pseudomonas fluorescens @ 5 g per litre as foliar application — effective biological control of Magnaporthe
Apply Trichoderma harzianum @ 4 g per litre as foliar spray — proven biological fungicide against rice blast
Spray Neem Oil 3000 PPM @ 3 ml per litre every 10 days from tillering — reduces spore germination on leaf surfaces
Use Cow Urine (fermented) @ 10% solution as foliar spray — traditional organic treatment with antifungal properties
treatment: Apply Tricyclazole 75% WP or Isoprothiolane 40% EC immediately
Spray at tillering stage for leaf blast control
Spray again at panicle initiation stage for neck blast control
Avoid excess nitrogen fertilization
Drain the field intermittently to reduce humidity

medication_timeline.when_to_apply: At tillering stage and panicle initiation
At tillering stage and panicle initiation
At tillering stage and panicle initiation

brownspot.cause_of_disease: Caused by Bipolaris oryzae, thriving in warm, humid conditions between 25–30°C. Strongly linked to potassium and silicon-deficient soils. Small circular to oval brown spots with a yellow halo appear on leaves, glumes, and grains. Weakened plants due to nutrient stress are most vulnerable.
deficiencies: Potassium Deficiency — primary predisposing factor; low soil potassium is directly linked to severe brown spot outbreaks in rice fields
Silicon Deficiency — silicon-deficient plants show dramatically increased susceptibility to brown spot lesion formation and spread
Nitrogen Deficiency — while excess nitrogen promotes disease, deficient nitrogen weakens plant immunity allowing the fungus to establish easily
disorders: Grain Discoloration Disorder — fungal infection of glumes and grains causes dark brown to black discoloration reducing market value significantly
Poor Grain Filling Disorder — severe brown spot reduces leaf area for photosynthesis causing poor carbohydrate supply to developing grains
Seedling Blight Disorder — in heavily infected seedbeds, the pathogen attacks seedlings causing damping-off and poor transplant establishment
natural_organic_treatment: Seed Treatment with Trichoderma viride @ 4 g per kg of seed before sowing — prevents seed-borne infection at germination
Apply potassium-rich organic amendment (wood ash @ 400 kg per acre) to correct soil potassium deficiency
Spray Neem Oil 3000 PPM @ 3 ml per litre every 10–14 days from early symptom appearance
Apply rice husk ash (silica source) @ 200 kg per acre as soil amendment to boost silicon levels and disease resistance
Use Pseudomonas fluorescens foliar spray @ 5 g per litre — biological suppression of Bipolaris oryzae spore germination
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP or Propiconazole 25% EC immediately
Apply potassium fertilizer to correct soil nutrient deficiency
Treat seeds with Thiram or Carbendazim before sowing as prevention
Remove and destroy infected stubble after harvest
Avoid water stress and maintain proper irrigation

tungro.viral_note: Viral Disease — No Direct Cure. Vector (leafhopper) control is the only management strategy.
tungro.cause_of_disease: Caused by Rice Tungro Bacilliform Virus (RTBV) and Rice Tungro Spherical Virus (RTSV), transmitted by the green leafhopper Nephotettix virescens. Infected plants show yellow-orange leaf discoloration, severely stunted growth, and reduced tillering. Warm, humid conditions and late transplanting increase infection risk.
deficiencies: Iron Deficiency Mimic — tungro-infected plants show iron-deficiency-like symptoms with interveinal chlorosis but it is caused by viral disruption of nutrient transport
Zinc Deficiency Mimic — the virus disrupts zinc uptake pathways causing symptoms resembling zinc deficiency such as stunted growth and yellowing
Nitrogen Utilization Disorder — even with adequate soil nitrogen, virus-infected plants cannot effectively absorb and utilize nitrogen causing pale yellow discoloration
disorders: Severe Stunting Disorder — infected plants stop growing at 30–50% of normal height making them unproductive and prone to shading by healthy plants
Spikelet Sterility Disorder — tungro-infected panicles produce mostly empty or partially-filled grains causing near-complete yield failure
Root Dysfunction Disorder — viral infection impairs root development and function reducing water and nutrient absorption even in well-fertilized fields
natural_organic_treatment: Spray Neem Oil 3000 PPM @ 5 ml per litre every 7 days to control leafhopper vector population naturally
Apply Yellow Sticky Traps @ 10–15 per acre to capture adult leafhoppers and monitor population levels
Use Reflective Mulches or silver-colored plastic mulch in nursery beds — disorients and deters leafhopper movement
Plant Trap Crops like Napier grass around field borders to attract and trap leafhoppers away from rice
Remove and destroy all infected plants immediately — this is the single most important organic management step to prevent virus spread
treatment: Apply Imidacloprid 17.8% SL immediately to control leafhopper vector
Apply Carbofuran 3% G in soil at transplanting as a preventive measure
Remove and destroy all infected plants immediately to prevent spread
Avoid late transplanting which increases leafhopper exposure
Use tungro-resistant rice varieties for future planting

medication_timeline.when_to_apply: At transplanting (preventive) and at first leafhopper detection

healthy.label: Healthy
healthy.message: No Disease Detected. Crop is Healthy.

mosiac.viral_note: Viral Disease — No Direct Cure. Aphid vector control and removal of infected plants is the only management strategy.
mosiac.cause_of_disease: Caused by Sugarcane Mosaic Virus (SCMV), transmitted by aphids especially Melanaphis sacchari. Leaves show alternating light green and dark green mosaic or streaking patterns. Spreads through infected seed cane and aphid movement. Warm, dry conditions with high aphid populations increase infection risk.
deficiencies: Nitrogen Deficiency — viral infection severely impairs nitrogen metabolism causing systemic protein breakdown and pale, mottled foliage
Potassium Deficiency — mosaic virus disrupts potassium transport leading to reduced stalk cell turgor and lower sugar accumulation
Magnesium Deficiency — virus-induced chlorophyll breakdown causes magnesium release symptoms including interveinal yellowing on older leaves
disorders: Reduced Sugar Content Disorder — SCMV disrupts photosynthesis and sugar metabolism in infected stalks directly reducing sucrose content and juice quality
Poor Ratoon Performance Disorder — virus persists in stubble and ratoon crops consistently show worse symptoms and lower yields than plant crop
Stunted Stalk Development Disorder — infected plants produce short, thin stalks with reduced internode length severely impacting commercial biomass
natural_organic_treatment: Spray Neem Oil 3000 PPM @ 5 ml per litre every 10–14 days to control aphid vector population naturally
Apply Garlic Extract spray @ 50 g crushed garlic in 1 litre water diluted 1:10 — natural repellent for aphid vectors
Use Yellow Sticky Traps @ 10 per acre to capture and monitor winged aphids before they establish colonies
Rogue out and destroy infected plants immediately on detection — prevents virus from spreading to healthy neighboring plants
Use certified disease-free seed cane — the single most effective organic prevention strategy for mosaic virus
treatment: Apply Imidacloprid 17.8% SL or Thiamethoxam 25% WG immediately to control aphid vector
Remove and destroy all infected plant stools immediately
Use only disease-free certified seed cane for planting
Rogue out infected plants promptly to prevent spread to healthy plants
Disinfect cutting tools with bleach solution between plants

medication_timeline.when_to_apply: At first aphid detection or early mosaic symptoms
At first aphid detection or early mosaic symptoms

redrot.cause_of_disease: Caused by Colletotrichum falcatum, the most serious disease of sugarcane. Thrives in warm, humid conditions between 28–32°C. Internal reddening of stalk with white patches and an alcoholic smell. Spreads through infected seed setts, waterlogged soils, and contaminated cutting tools. Most damaging during ratoon crop stage.
deficiencies: Calcium Deficiency — weakens cell wall structure in stalk tissue making it easier for the fungus to penetrate and colonize internal tissues
Boron Deficiency — impairs cell wall cross-linking and reduces the plant's ability to produce defensive phenolic compounds against the pathogen
Potassium Deficiency — reduces stalk tissue density and osmotic regulation making the plant more susceptible to fungal colonization
disorders: Internal Stalk Rot Disorder — the fungus destroys internal stalk tissue causing complete hollowing with characteristic red discoloration and white patches
Fermentation Disorder — destroyed stalk tissue undergoes fermentation producing alcoholic smell which is a diagnostic sign of advanced red rot infection
Ratoon Failure Disorder — infected stumps and stubble harbor the fungus throughout the season causing complete failure of ratoon crop establishment
natural_organic_treatment: Hot Water Sett Treatment @ 52°C for 30 minutes before planting — highly effective organic method to kill fungus on seed setts without chemicals
Sett Treatment with Trichoderma viride @ 4 g per litre — soak setts for 30 minutes in bio-fungicide solution before planting
Apply Neem Cake @ 200 kg per acre as soil amendment — suppresses soil-borne Colletotrichum falcatum population naturally
Drench soil with Pseudomonas fluorescens @ 5 g per litre around plant base to suppress fungal activity in root zone
Remove and burn infected clumps immediately — do not compost infected material as the fungus survives composting
treatment: Treat seed setts by soaking in Carbendazim 50% WP solution for 30 minutes before planting
Apply Propiconazole 25% EC or Hexaconazole 5% EC as field spray at early symptoms
Remove and burn all infected clumps immediately
Avoid waterlogging by improving field drainage
Disinfect all cutting tools with bleach before use

rust.cause_of_disease: Caused by Puccinia melanocephala (Brown Rust) or Puccinia kuehnii (Orange Rust). Spreads through airborne spores in warm, humid conditions between 25–30°C. Small, orange-brown pustules appear on underside of leaves. Wind is the primary carrier. Dense planting and excessive irrigation create ideal conditions for rapid spread.
deficiencies: Silicon Deficiency — silicon-deficient plants lack the epidermal cell wall reinforcement needed to resist rust pustule formation and spore penetration
Potassium Deficiency — weakens the plant's systemic acquired resistance reducing its ability to produce anti-rust defense compounds
Sulphur Deficiency — low sulphur impairs the plant's synthesis of defensive proteins and phytoalexins that naturally suppress rust infection
disorders: Leaf Chlorosis Disorder — extensive rust pustule formation causes massive chlorophyll breakdown leading to large-scale yellowing of leaf tissue
Premature Leaf Drying Disorder — heavy rust infection causes leaves to dry from tip to base reducing the green photosynthetic area by 40–60%
Reduced Sugar Recovery Disorder — photosynthesis loss from rust-damaged leaves directly reduces sucrose synthesis and juice sugar content at harvest
natural_organic_treatment: Spray Neem Oil 3000 PPM @ 5 ml per litre every 10–14 days from first pustule detection
Apply Wettable Sulphur (organic grade) @ 3 g per litre — effective natural contact fungicide specifically against rust pathogens
Use Bacillus subtilis bio-fungicide spray @ 2 g per litre as foliar spray — proven biological control of rust in sugarcane
Apply Silicon-rich soil amendment (rice husk ash @ 200 kg per acre) — boosts systemic resistance against rust infection
Avoid dense planting and trim lower leaves to improve air circulation and reduce the humid microclimate that favors rust
treatment: Apply Propiconazole 25% EC or Triadimefon 25% WP immediately at early pustule stage
Avoid overly dense planting to reduce humidity in the canopy
Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves
Use rust-resistant sugarcane varieties for future planting
Apply preventive fungicide sprays during high humidity season

yellow.viral_note: Viral Disease — No Direct Cure. Aphid vector control and use of certified disease-free planting material is the only management strategy.
yellow.cause_of_disease: Caused by Sugarcane Yellow Leaf Virus (ScYLV), transmitted by the sugarcane aphid Melanaphis sacchari. Yellowing begins on the midrib on the underside of leaves and progresses to full leaf yellowing. Warm, dry weather with high aphid populations accelerates spread. Causes 10–30% yield loss.
deficiencies: Potassium Deficiency Mimic — ScYLV disrupts potassium transport causing midrib yellowing symptoms that strongly resemble potassium deficiency
Magnesium Deficiency — virus disrupts chlorophyll synthesis by impairing magnesium incorporation causing progressive leaf yellowing from midrib outward
Nitrogen Utilization Failure — even in nitrogen-adequate soils, virus-infected plants cannot process nitrogen efficiently causing systemic yellowing and stunting
disorders: Midrib Yellowing Disorder — characteristic yellow discoloration of the underside of leaf midrib is the earliest and most diagnostic symptom of ScYLV infection
Photosynthetic Collapse Disorder — progressive yellowing of the entire leaf destroys chlorophyll causing massive reduction in photosynthesis and sugar production
Poor Juice Quality Disorder — infected stalks show significantly reduced sucrose content, increased reducing sugars, and poor juice purity at extraction
natural_organic_treatment: Spray Neem Oil 3000 PPM @ 5 ml per litre every 10 days — effectively controls the aphid vector Melanaphis sacchari population
Apply Garlic + Chilli Extract spray (50 g garlic + 25 g dry chilli per litre, diluted 1:10) — natural aphid repellent spray
Apply potassium-rich organic compost @ 2 tonnes per acre — helps compensate for virus-induced potassium utilization disruption
Use Yellow Sticky Traps @ 12–15 per acre to monitor and capture winged aphid vectors before they spread the virus
Remove and destroy all severely infected plants immediately — reduces the virus reservoir available for aphid-mediated spread
treatment: Apply Imidacloprid 17.8% SL or Dimethoate 30% EC immediately to control aphid vector
Use only virus-free certified planting material
Remove and destroy all severely infected plants immediately
Apply balanced potassium fertilization to support plant recovery
Monitor aphid population regularly using yellow sticky traps
